sentences.text: Yes, it's a street called Mila.
And they made one of the biggest selections in Warsaw Ghetto that because I can't give you numbers of people.
But let's say, if 50,000 people went through the selection, let's say 5,000 came out, and the 45,000 was sent to the Treblinka, you understand?
That's why I want to tell you about it.
My father was the only person from our family who went to the selection.
He put us in a bunker, in an attic.
He took out bricks.
And they put us in the attic.
There were about 100 people there, a lot of people.
And they-- my father was the only one that went out to the selection.
If he wouldn't have come back, I hate to see what would have happened to us.
But he did come back.
And while the selection went on, I think, for a couple of days, or a day, I don't remember how long.
And we were sitting there.
And the Germans came.
And they were looking through all the apartments.
We heard their boots and we heard their voices.
And they were looking for other apartments-- in other apartments looking for Jewish people, you know.
They didn't go to the selection in Mila Street.
And they were very close to our attic.
And we were all holding our breath.
We had little children with us.
But somehow, or rather luckily, they went away, they didn't find our bunk yet.
And then my father came and took out the bricks and we all went out.
And we had to smuggle ourselves at nighttime from Uklidova to Leszno to go back with the people who were already selected, who were allowed to remain life stayed.
You see, they didn't come back to Uklidova.
And my father was a hero because he went, he didn't know whether he was going to go to gas or not.
He was not selected to go to the Treblinka.
He was lucky, you know, at the time.
And he took us back.
And we smuggled ourselves to Leszno, 76.
And my parents had friends there who-- things were getting really grimmer and grimmer.
There were two young couples, professional.
They were lawyers.
And one morning, my mother-- I heard my mother saying to my father, they're not there anymore.
They committed suicide, all four of them.
There were two sisters with two husbands.
A lot of people were committing suicide it Warsaw Ghetto at the time, because people already knew what was going on.
Also, my parents had a friend whose husband ran away from Treblinka.
He was chosen to work, you know, with the clothing from that people.
And he smuggled himself into the clothing, and came back on a train with the clothing, and ran out from the train through the window.
And somehow or other, a peasant-- peasants looked after him, Polish peasants.
And he made his way back to Warsaw Ghetto and to his wife and told them, told everybody what they're doing with people in Treblinka.
Oh yes.
Treblinka was--
Oh yes.
I told you, we knew from the time that this Mr.
Francis' wife and Mr.
Leonard's wife and the little girl were taken from Ogrodowa 29.
That was before the selection.
Anyway, as I told you, the selection was very few Jewish people left.
And everybody was very, very petrified.
And we knew it won't be very much longer when they will get us, too.
And sure enough, my father decided that the time has come for us to go to our bunker.
When we go to work, he said, now we're going to go to our bunker and not come out any more.
The bunker that we had in Ogrodowa 29 prepared for us because he could feel already that things were going to go bad for us, as well.
But the catch, you see, it was coming to Pesach.
And my father said, and my mother, let's be together with Jewish people during Pesach.
And straight after Pesach, we'll go to our bunker and won't come out anymore.
Yes, it was 1943 in April.
And that's when my parents made their mistake.
I don't know whether they would have survived, anyhow.
But that was a mistake because the Germans used to do all the most terrible things to Jewish people on Jewish holidays.
And they started the action which was called Judenrein.
Have you ever come across the word Judenrein?
Yes, the Judenrein means the final cleansing of the Jews.
And that's when they started, at the first seder night.
And my father went to the-- there were, you know, like borders.
I don't know, yes, well, not borders, like what would you call it?
There was a German policeman, a Jewish policeman, and an SS-man standing near the border of the Ghetto to the Polish side.
And my father went to them and offered them a lot of money to let us go to the Ogrodowa 29.
I don't know how they didn't kill him, but they didn't.
And my father spoke a perfect German.
And the SS spoke to him and he said to him, you came one day too late.
I cannot let you through anymore.
So here we were now without a bunker.
And in the [? flood ?], we were leaving.
There were more and more people because they were all the time closing together or making this smaller and smaller.
And I remember that the Jewish people had bunkers all over the place-- attics, cellars, everywhere.
And some very nice people, I remember, were eating matzo.
It was Pesach.
And some very nice people took us into a bunker.
It was a most difficult place to get into the bunker.
What they did was they bricked up a room and all the floors.
And this bunker you only went to this one room.
But so the Germans won't know that there is another room in each apartment.
They bricked it up.
And we had to go through an oven, you know.
And we stayed there, but not very long, because somebody came and said, get out of here quickly.
They put the house on fire.
So we started running.
You know, in Warsaw and in Lodz, too, the attics on the-- on the, you know, on the upper floor, there were meetings, you know, like open from one attic to another, from one house to another.
So we started running from this house because it was on fire.
And we had fire in front of us and fire in back of us.
There was a big things, you know, like big wooden things falling off the building, and with fire in front of us.
And we were petrified, just the four of us were running, you know, absolutely petrified.
And at one stage, we found ourselves on a roof.
And the roof was a pitched roof.
And we were on the fifth or sixth floor.
And I remember the terrible fear.
I thought I'm going to fall down.
But eventually we came to another house.
And in that house, we got another bunker.
People let us into the bunker.
And there was a terrible, terrible lot of shooting going, screaming and shooting and shooting.
And we found out that somebody came and said, you know that the Jewish young people have started an uprising.
They are fighting the Germans.
And it was really hard to believe, you know.
But they did, they fought them for five or six weeks, you know.
And I can't even tell you how heroic that was because such countries like France and-- what do I know-- Belgium or even Poland, they all fell.
It was in days, weeks, where they had such armies.
And here, these few young Jewish kids, they had Molotov cocktails and things like that.
Anyway, we got into this bunker, it was in the cellar.
And it was a lot of people there.
And I remember a few things about this bunker.
There was no water to drink, the water was real rust, it was yellow.
And there was a little baby there, a newborn baby.
And the baby was crying all the time.
And the people kept saying, you have to kill the baby, you have to kill the baby.
Because if you won't kill the baby, they will find us.
And believe it or not, they killed the baby.
They killed the baby because it was 100 lives that had perhaps a chance of survival against one life that had absolutely no chance of survival.
I don't know how they did it, but they killed this baby.
And then it was very, very quiet.
And we were sitting there.
And it was a terrible, terrible time, because we were all so scared.
We were already halfway de-humanized, I think.
I'm sure we were.
And all of a sudden, the manhole opened up and we saw the guns pointed our way.
And we thought, well that's it, you know, the Germans have come to get us.
[LAUGHS] Believe it or not, it was the Warsaw Ghetto fighters, the young people came in.
I will never forget that as long as I live.
They were very young, they were 16, 17, 18.
They had those caps, you know, and they had guns.
And they were telling us what's happening, how they are fighting the Germans, they have killed a lot of Germans, and the Germans are coming with tanks.
They're fighting them like a real army, you know.
And [SIGHS] from that bunker, we had to go, too, because they put this house on fire, too.
And going back to the Warsaw Ghetto-- kids, you know.
To this very day, I feel that it's like I have witnessed a part of Jewish history, a very important, a vital part of Jewish history, because I think that it has given dignity, courage.
You see, they always say that we went like lambs to slaughter, but we did not.
Because this was something, that it was against all odds.
Do you understand?
The Poles would not help the Jews with weapons.
And the Jews were on their own to try to-- it was underground, they tried to.
But they had to pay a lot of money for every gun they got.
And I think they fought against such odds for six weeks, that the Germans-- there were  a lot of Germans.
I'm repeating myself.
The Germans rode tanks, you understand that?
Anyway, they put this-- they burned this house, too.
And now, my uncle and his wife and my little cousin got-- we got together and we started running.
And we ran to a house in a different street where it was empty.
You see, there were no Jewish people there.
It was completely empty.
And they had a commander that came and cleared up completely these flats.
Not for cleanliness sake, but they robbed it, you know.
They took every piece of furniture, everything, and they sent it to Germany.
And they had a commander called [GERMAN].
What they did was they took the [NON-ENGLISH] covers, and sent it to Germany and left the feathers.
And anyway, there was my-- the four of us and my uncle and my little cousin and auntie.
And two ladies attached themselves to us, and a man.
I don't-- I don't know how many, I never counted how many we were.
But we got-- we must have met the other people in that flat.
We got to the flat and it was empty.
And there was nothing there except the feathers.
And we were sitting there.
By that time, we didn't eat anything for three days because we didn't have any food at all, none of us.
And all of a sudden, we hear this German marching from flat to flat.
So we hid underneath the feathers, all of us.
And the Germans came to the apartment and they didn't see us and they went away.
And my mother said, oh, aren't we lucky they didn't get us.
But half an hour later, they came again and it was really lucky that they did find us.
One of the ladies foot was protruding from the feathers.
And they realized that there are people there and they pull out the guns and they said, get out of there and stand up.
And we did.
And they took us all down, and the house was on fire.
The whole street, house after house after house, was on fire.
If they wouldn't have found us, we would have burned alive.
They took us across the road and they put us against a fence.
And that was already [GERMAN].
We were the last people-- one of the last people to leave Warsaw.
It was-- by that time it was May 1943.
And they asked my father and my uncle to show them what the Jewish people are hiding, in what bunkers.
And my father said to them, look, if I knew the bunkers, I would be myself in a bunker.
I don't know any bunkers.
And the other man said, I'll take you.
He didn't know, but he was so scared that he went.
They took away my uncle and my father.
But somehow or other, I don't-- I'll ever see them again.
But an hour or so later, they came back.
And the other men didn't.
They terrorized us, something that I can't even describe to you.
And my mother had-- you wouldn't believe it, my mother had a whole kilo of gold.
That was the only thing we possessed.
And you know, she was taking it with her, because we didn't know where we were going to go.
Maybe she thought we can pay for our lives with this gold.
And of course, they took it away, straightaway, as we were standing there.
And I had a little thing with a ruby that I could never take off my finger.
But they took it off, maybe because I didn't eat for three days and I lost weight.
Well, Umschlagplatz was the biggest hell on earth.
I have never ever-- I mean, later on, I went through all different kind of hells.
But this was probably one of the biggest hells I went through.
They packed everybody.
It was a building, it was next to the railway.
And they packed everybody in because-- and they took little-- people to little cubicles and they used to beat them up.
They took my mother to a cubicle and my father to cubicle.
And I thought I'll never see them again.
I was petrified because I was a child, my brother was even younger.
And-- but they came back.
And we were sitting on the floor.
And every-- still only had a handful.
We ate-- got one onion from somebody, who gave us an onion, and because we didn't eat anything for three days.
And one woman gave us a bit of dried noodles.
No, we had absolutely no water.
And all the time they were coming and demanding gold from the people.
And they put blankets.
And they said, you put the gold on the blankets and people were giving them whatever they had, rings.
But eventually, they didn't have anything more to give, you know.
And they were beating people constantly up and raping women.
And two girls threw themselves out of the windows.
Oh, yes.
And the people who took part in it were Lithuanians and Latvians and Estonians, not only Germans anymore.
They were all wearing black uniforms and they were absolutely and totally inhuman.

They were doing it with such a-- it was the Germans-- they were doing it was such a laugh, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like I wouldn't kill a bed bug with a laugh like that, you know, because I would have pity.
I would only kill it because it's a nuisance to me.
But they were doing it with such a laugh, I can't even tell you.
It was such a cruelty and such a sadism.
Eventually, they put us in trains, in cattle trains.
I was maybe a day and a night in Umschlagplatz, sitting in that hall, in the big hall, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably a day and a night.
I really can't be very accurate.
But to me, it seemed like a long-- very long time.
And they put us in cattle trains.
And people were suffocating.
The windows were very little.
I was still with my parents.
Yes, but by that time we lost my uncle and my auntie.
We didn't see them anymore.
I don't know what happened to them.
I was still with my father and my mother and my brother.
And people were undressing themselves naked in the train.
It was so hot, there was no water.
And people were dying there.
It was an absolute nightmare.
A nightmare is not the expression for it.
I don't have it in my vocabulary what it was.
Pardon?
Oh yes, there were guards all the time, you know.
You mean German guards?
Yes, there were, as we were boarding the train.
At every carriage, there was a German guard with a big dog standing there.
Eventually, we came to Lublin.
No, I don't remember.
But we came to Lublin.
And Lublin was the next big city to Majdanek.
And in Lublin, my father, again-- we were dying of thirst, but absolutely dying of thirst.
And my father went to a German guard and offered him money for water.
We had, we still, he must have-- yeah, we had money sewn in.
I forgot to tell you, forgot to tell you.
We had money sewn in on the collars, which they didn't take because they didn't know about it.
So I'll come to it later.
And he offered him money.
And you wouldn't believe it, he brought a bottle of water.
I don't know how much money he gave him, a lot of money.
And he brought a bottle of water for us.
So that was the first water we drank, I don't know, in how many days.
When we-- after that, there was a lot of people and they started, they said, women separately and men separately.
So my father went with my brother and I went with my mother.
That was the last time that I have seen my father or my brother.
I'm sure we said goodbye.
I'm more than sure we said goodbye.
I'm sure we kissed and said [INAUDIBLE].
And my mother and I were marched.
The women were all marched off and for some mysterious reason-- well, now when I look back, it wasn't a mysterious reason, it was fate-- were put into rows of five.
And I don't know, they put my mother in first in a row in front of me.
And I was in the row in the back because they were throwing us into rows, you understand.
They were creating the rows and throwing us into the rows.
And my mother was in the row in front of me, I was behind.
And there were counting people, 100.
And they cut off my mother from me.
They counted 100, like 20 rows, and my mother marched off.
And here I was left in the first row, a child on its own, without a father, without a mother, without my brother.
And I was standing there, and with the other people.
A lot of people knew us because they were working with us and living in Leszno, some people knew us.
And there were terrible, terrible mud there.
It was horrible.
And I lost my shoe.
We stayed there for a while.
And then they started marching us, they count another 100 and started marching.
And a man in a striped suit noticed that I have lost one shoe in the mud.
And he ran quickly-- no, he was perhaps 100 meters away, there were barracks.
And he ran quickly and threw the shoes at me, a new pair of shoes.
And I grabbed the shoes and they fitted me.
And they marched us, and they marched us to a place which later on I found was a sauna.
And before we marched to the sauna, there was one SS-man sitting here and one SS-man sitting there, and he made a selection.
And he put all the children and women and old people to one side, and all young women to other side.
Now I remember what my father said, you do the opposite what the Germans tell you to do.
And also by that time, I knew that the children and all the people would be gassed.
By the time, we knew, what-- that they were gassing the Jewish people.
So I-- he put me as the first.
SS-man put me together with the children and the women on this side.
And I smuggled myself out and I went to the second SS-man, I thought maybe he won't do that, maybe he would put me with the young people.
How old were you?
[SIGHS] Well, I was, I think, 11 and 1/2 or 12, I don't know exactly. 1943-- that's four years.
I was [INAUDIBLE] it must have been 12 years.
And I would have been 12 in probably in October.
That was in May.
And I smuggled myself to the second SS-man.
And he did the same, he put me with women and children.
And they marched us off to a place surrounded with wires.
And there were only women and children.
And all of a sudden, from far away, I see my mother marching out of the sauna.
And I'm screaming to my mother, mama, mama, I'm going to my death-- in Polish of course.
And my mother said-- she didn't hear.
And she said to me, Lusia, she said, Lusia, tell the lady who gives the frocks to give you a warm frock.
And she marched off.
Now there were two guards at the door, at the gate, you know, seeing that nobody got out of this big yard.
Well, I was going all the time to these two guards, saying to them, [GERMAN], which is a broken German saying, I am 16 years old and I can work.
And they were pushing me with their guns away all the time, you know.
I went three or four times.
They were pushing me all the time.
And somehow, I-- I-- when I was saying to my mother, we're going to-- I'm going to my death, those women who knew me said, why are you saying such terrible things.
Because of you, they will send us to death.
And I looked at them, I, a 12-year-old child, looked at them and I said, we are going to our deaths, anyhow.
And I kept going to these two guards.
And, you know, all of a sudden they started smoking cigarettes and they put their guns on the ground.
And a man in a striped suit again ran into the big yard with the people, you know, women and children, grabbed me by my hand and spoke to me in Czechoslovakian.
I never ever heard Czechoslovakian spoken, but I understood somehow what he said to me.
He said, I'm going to bring you to the sauna, which is people that were already selected to go to work were.
And you are to-- there is going to be another selection, he said.
You are to stand right at the very end, at the wall, so that they won't to see you.
And he left me.
And I went in.
There was a selection naked.
And we were standing in fives, like this.
And I was the last one.
And they came again and made a selection.
But they didn't see me because I was the last one, the fifth one, and a little one, you know.
Somehow, they didn't see me.
And the women that were giving clothes, they were Polish women, and they were nice.
And they realized what was happening, that I'm a child.
They quickly gave me a frock.
I was the first one to get a frock so it would cover up that I'm a child and I'm flat-- flat-chested, you know.
And they marched me to Majdanek camp.
And that was a difference of quite a many, many hours.
And I went to this barrack, and my mother was there.
And my mother said, what happened to you.
She said, I was so frantic, I was asking everybody where you were.
And they kept saying, oh, you're going to come, you're going to come.
But they knew that I was going-- I was put to gas.
So this man that I have never ever seen before or after, he saved me.
Out for years and years, I was thinking how come he came to me when there were so many people there, you know, children and women.
And a few years ago, I found out, it occurred to me that I must have drawn his attention to me by just constant going to the German guards and saying [GERMAN].
And those people-- he was a Jewish man, you know.
They were working there, he must have noticed that.
So when there was smoking a cigarette, he smuggled me out.
And I was three months in Majdanek with my mother.
And there were selections all the time.
In a barrack, on a-- on a-- they were not [INAUDIBLE], they were bunk-- bunks, different to-- to Birkenau.
There were-- there was a terrible lot of lice there.
And every day, they were snatching people to go and work in a place called [GERMAN], which was a garden.
I was very lucky, you know, in that camp.
Because I was a child, people took pity on me.
And the camp was run by Polish women, not Jewish.
And the Polish women were very cruel to the Jewish women.
But somehow, they were nice to me.
And I don't know why, they kept saying, how old are you.
And I kept saying, I'm 16.
So they used to say, you're 16 like we are 80.
And that's how it was, you know.
I kept saying I'm older.
And my mother got me a long frock.
And she braided my hair to make me look older.
And those women were helping me, the Polish women, sometimes.
I had a Blockalteste, a Polish woman.
And every time there was a selection, she used to hide me.
And I'll tell you how she was hiding me.
